coredata.dc:title: Saffron derived carbon quantum dot/N-doped ZnO/fulvic acid nanocomposite for sonocatalytic degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Moalem-Banhangi, Monire
Ghaeni, Nayereh
Ghasemi, Shahram
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, N-doped ZnO/fulvic acid (FA)/carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanocomposite was prepared through hydrothermal method for catalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). The CQD nanoparticles were synthesized from dried stigma of saffron. FT-IR, XRD, TGA, BET, FESEM-EDS, and TEM were conducted to characterize the prepared nanocomposite. A sonocatalytic approach using probe-type ultrasonic irradiation (20 kHz, 100 W) was applied to treat polluted water with MB. The synergetic capability of ultrasonic irradiation and catalytic activity of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite produce reactive species for better degradation of MB in aqueous environments. Based on the results, the optimized values for main parameters in pH of 8 were determined as N-ZnO/FA/CQD dosage of 1.5 g/L, MB primary concentration of 20 mg/L, time= 50 min, and [H2O2]= 5 mM. The adsorption behavior of MB onto N-ZnO/FA/CQD was demonstrated through the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and can be fitted with Langmuir isotherm model. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite showed the enhanced sonocatalytic activity with degradation efficiency of 94% for MB dye. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite might be regarded as an efficient catalyst for degredation of organic dyes in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Saffron
Green synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
Fulvic acid
Zinc oxide
Degradation
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparative study on MB degradation by different adsorbents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the sonocatalytic degradation of MB in the presence of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposites.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) BJH plot of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite and (c) TGA of N-ZnO/CQD/FA nanocomposite.
(a) FESEM and (b) TEM images of N-ZnO//FA/CQD nanocomposite at different magnifications.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of the CQD and photograph of the CQD under UV–Vis light and (b) effect of nanoparticles types on sonocatalytic MB degradation. (c) Comparison of MB degradation efficiency in different times. The degradation conditions: [MB]0 = 20 mg/L, [Catalyst]= 1.5 g/L, pH 8, [H2O2]= 5 mM.

Effect of (a) pH, (b) dye concentration, (c) concentration H2O2, (d) time and (e) nanocatalyst dosage (g/L) on MB degradation efficiency. (f) Reusability of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite. The optimum degradation conditions: [MB]0 = 20 mg/L, [Catalyst]= 1.5 g/L, pH 8, time= 50 min and [H2O2]= 5 mM.
The BET surface area values of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite.
Isotherm parameters obtained from Langmuir and Freundlich.
Kinetic parameters for degradation of MB onto N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Saffron derived carbon quantum dot/N-doped ZnO/fulvic acid nanocomposite for sonocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, N-doped ZnO/fulvic acid (FA)/carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanocomposite was prepared through hydrothermal method for catalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). The CQD nanoparticles were synthesized from dried stigma of saffron. FT-IR, XRD, TGA, BET, FESEM-EDS, and TEM were conducted to characterize the prepared nanocomposite. A sonocatalytic approach using probe-type ultrasonic irradiation (20 kHz, 100 W) was applied to treat polluted water with MB. The synergetic capability of ultrasonic irradiation and catalytic activity of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite produce reactive species for better degradation of MB in aqueous environments. Based on the results, the optimized values for main parameters in pH of 8 were determined as N-ZnO/FA/CQD dosage of 1.5 g/L, MB primary concentration of 20 mg/L, time= 50 min, and [H2O2]= 5 mM. The adsorption behavior of MB onto N-ZnO/FA/CQD was demonstrated through the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and can be fitted with Langmuir isotherm model. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite showed the enhanced sonocatalytic activity with degradation efficiency of 94% for MB dye. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite might be regarded as an efficient catalyst for degredation of organic dyes in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays the existence of organic dyes in aqueous effluents caused by various industrial activities is a significant problem which is due to their stability in the environment and adverse impacts on aquatic and human health even at low proportions [1–3]. Methylene blue (MB) with the molecular formula of C16H18N3SCl is a heterocyclic aromatic chemical. MB has many applications in various fields, such as biology and chemistry [4] and is one of the several popular colorants for dyeing in the textile industry. In recent years, the different traditional measures are being adopted for the removal of organic compounds in pollution waters at the world [5–10]. Although these methods have vital role in water pollution control, complexity in reactor design and high energy consumption make them undesirable for large-scale promotion [11,12].

To overcome these limitations, advance oxidation processes (AOPs) have been considered which are characterized by the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) with strong oxidation capacity. Ultrasound irradiations are widely applied for the removal of organic dyes in aqueous media [13]. In this way, degradation is done by cavitation bubbles produced from the ultrasonic irradiation [14,15]. The generated bubbles with a very small lifetime of about microseconds, grow with ultrasound irradiation and finally collapse after arriving at a critical size [16]. The result of this implosion is the production of localized hot spots with high temperature and pressure. The unstable hot spots can generate hydrogen and hydroxyl reactive radicals from water molecules. Furthermore, the formed radicals interact with molecular structures of contaminants, which decompose them into less hazardous materials. The •OH radicals are strong oxidizing agents that are capable of destroying and converting nearly all persistent organic pollutants to CO2 and H2O through their complete mineralization. In addition, the efficiency of the sonochemical oxidation manner can be increased through adding chemicals such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and nanoparticles. The cavitation effect plays imprortant role for successful ultrasonic degradation process, and there are widespread studies related to it [17,18]. Introduction of a solid catalyst into the solution improves the generation of •OH radicals since it enhances the formation of hot spots by increasing the number of nuclei that are available for the production of cavitational bubbles [19]. In the sonocatalytic process with the presence of a suitable catalyst, the efficiency of the dye-pollutant decomposition can be enhanced, and this is likely due to a synergistic effect between the ultrasonic irradiation and the solid semiconductor catalyst.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) is one of the useful sonocatalysts, because ZnO is a wide bandgap semiconductor (3.37 eV) with a 60 meV exciton binding energy, which is attractive for potential use in different technologies. However, ZnO sonocatalytical efficiency is greatly hindered because quick recombination and the narrow absorption band of ZnO sonocatalyst [20–24]. To overwhelm these issues, the modification of semiconductor sonocatalysts with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is of considerable significance owing to their advantages like photo stability, non-toxic, heavy metal-free, low cost, and eco-friendly in nature [25,26]. Similar to other members of the carbon material family (e.g., carbon nanotube, graphene), enhancing the adsorption capacity toward reaction substrates is another important and fundamental role of CQDs. Generally, depending on the specific reaction, the adsorption-interaction types of target reactants (e.g., heavy metals, dyes, and aromatic compounds) on CQDs-based composites are different, which can be attributed to physical adsorption, electrostatic attraction, or chemical interaction [27]. The aromatic regions of CQDs can form a π-πstacking interaction with organic pollutants containing aromatic structures, thereby improving the adsorption of reaction substrates. In addition, the presence of functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, epoxy, hydroxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of CQDs enables photocatalysts to interact with a wide variety of molecules and metal ions and thus contributing to improving the adsorption of reaction substrates [28,29].

In recent years, new methods based on green synthesis were developed as a carbon source for the synthesis of carbon dots. Saffron is one of the most important Iran export products. Iran as the original home of saffron has always had the largest harvest area. Saffron has been used as effective raw materials for the preparation of numerous bio-derived products owing to their plentiful availability, structural aspects and eco-friendly nature. Saffron contains volatile and non-volatile metabolites that cause its distinguished properties.

Fulvic acid (FA) used in synthesis of nanocomposite is a mixture of weak aliphatic and aromatic organic acids that form naturally in the decomposed organic matter of soil called humus. FA contains various functional groups such as OH, COOH and phenolic acids ( Fig. 1) which its high surface sites provide appropriate conditions for adsorption of MB [30–32].

The aim of this study is synthesis of novel nanocomposite (N-ZnO/FA/CQD) with the use of the low-cost, available and natural material. Hydrophilic and highly stable CQDs were also obtained from Saffron using hydrothermal method. The synthesis of nanocomposite has many benefits such as economical carbon source, single-step route, and simple process without the addition of any chemical reagents. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was used to catalytic degradation of MB under ultrasonic irradiation. The parameters that can influence on the performance of catalyst during the degradation process have been optimized to find the highest efficiency.

Pure saffron was purchased from Khorasan province (Iran). For the synthesis of the CQD, a uniform powder was obtained from the dried stigma of saffron. Briefly, 0.50 g powder of saffron was added in 100 mL deionized water and stirred for 15 min. In the following, the product was transferred into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. At the end of the reaction, the final mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the dark brown mixture was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 15 min to remove any large particles. Finally, the sample was dried using the freeze-drying method. The CQD mixture is stable and it was filed in a dark place at 4 °C [33,34].

Briefly, 1 g of FA was added into 80 mL deionized water and dispersed using ultrasonic for 30 min. Then Zn(NO3)2·4H2O (20 mM) was added into aqueous solution. 2 mL ammonia solution (32%) was added drop-wise to keep the pH of solution around 11. The solution was moved into an autoclave and heated at 90°C for 3 h. The final product was filtered by centrifuge, washed with distilled water several times, and dried at 50°C for 10 h. This method was replicated for the preparation of pure ZnO without FA [35].

The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was prepared as follows: 50 mg N-ZnO/FA and 10 mg CQD were dispersed in 30 mL ethanol for 40 min at 25°C. The N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was separated through filtration, washed with ethanol three times, and finally dried at 40°C for 10 h.

The structure of the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips, Cu kα radiation; λ = 0.154 nm). To determine the functional groups in the nanocomposite, Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 27) was used. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite were recorded using a JEOL microscope operating at 100 kV. Morphological and structural analysis of the nanocomposite were investigated using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Mira3 Tescan, Czech Republic) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA, Perkin-Elmer Diamond TG analyzer) was applied to monitor the thermal stability of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite.

Sonocatalytic performance of adsorbent was investigated for the MB degradation. In a typical degradation process, 25 mL of MB solution (10–40 mg/L) was assigned to a 50 mL double wall glass cell and a certain amount of the catalyst was added and H2O2 concentration was adjusted at 5 mM. The solution was irradiated using a probe-type ultrasonic irradiation system (20 kHz, 100 W) for 50 min. The temperature of the reaction was fixed to 25 °C with the circulatory cooling operation (Julabo f 34) during the experiments. After adsorption, the adsorbent particles were separated from the dye solution by centrifugation and then remnant MB concentration was defined by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco V-750) ( Fig. 2). The pH of the solution was regulated by adding NaOH (1 M) or HCl (1 M).

The adsorption capacity of MB on the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was calculated through Eq. (1) and the percentage of the dye degradation rate is calculated by Eq. (2):
(1)
q
e
=
(
C
i
−
C
e
)
×
V m
(2)
R =
C
i
−
C
e
C
i
× 100
Where, qe is the amount of MB per unit weight of adsorbent (mg/g) and V is the volume of solution (L) and m is the dry mass of the nanocomposite (g). R is MB degradation rate, Ci and Ce are the initial and equilibrium dye concentrations (mg/L).

XRD technique was studied to characterize the crystalline structures and purity of the materials. Fig. 3a shows the XRD patternes of ZnO and N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite. All of the diffraction peaks can be indexed as the hexagonal phase of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36-1451). The characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 2ϴ values of 31.7, 33.4 and 36.2° represent the (100), (002), and (101) crystal planes of ZnO [15]. In addition, the XRD pattern of the nanocomposite confirms the crystalline structure of the nanocomposite. The peaks of the FA overlap with the ZnO characteristic peaks [33]. A broad diffraction peak at 2ϴ of 26° attributing to graphitic (002) peak with regular characteristic peaks of ZnO and FA are observed in Fig. 3a, denoting that the CQDs were added to the ZnO/FA particles [35,36].

The FTIR analysis indicates the appearance of the functional groups in the sample. Fig. 3b shows the FT-IR of ZnO/CQD/FA nanocomposites. The relatively intense band at 3500 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of O–H or N-H and the peaks found between 2800 and 2900 cm−1 originate from the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of C–H bonds [30]. The peak at 1100–1200 cm−1 area indicates the appearance of OC-OH in FA. The broad band at 1100–1200 cm−1 is probably due to the C˭C scissoring. In addition, the band at 1650 cm−1 determines the C˭O stretching, which arises from the behavior of carboxylic folvate anion in N-ZnO/FA/CQD composite [37]. A sharp peak at 575 cm−1 is perceived for the Zn–O stretching in ZnO of nanocomposite [33]. To give more information, the FT-IR spectra of pure FA, ZnO, ZnO/FA and CQD are also provided in Fig. S1. The spectrum of nanocomposite contains the functional groups of FA and CQD. In addition, in spectrum of ZnO, the Zn–O stretching can be observed.

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis and the BJH plot for N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite are show in Fig. 4a and b. The results indicate that N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite shows a type IV isotherm with small hysteresis loop (H3 type) at high P/P0 ratio. As can be seen from Table 1, the porous material has BET specific surface area and the total pore volume of 16.099 m2 g−1 and 3.698 cm3 g−1, respectively [38]. In addition, from BJH plot, the mean pore diameter was obtained 1.21 nm for the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite.

Fig. 4c shows TGA analysis of N-ZnO/FA/CQD composites. As can be seen, the mass of nanocomposite decrease to 60% of the initial value at 600°C. It means that the nanocomposite has some organic compounds in the structure. The initial weight loss around 100°C is due to the adsorbed water molecules in the structure and other steps are due to the gradual decomposition of FA and CQD.

Fig. 5a shows the FESEM images of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite at different magnification. The sample displays nanoparticles with a spherical-like form with an average size of 30 nm. The TEM images show the synthesis of rod-like shape ZnO (Fig. 5b at different magnifications). The results of EDS analysis for the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 6, which displays the presence of C, O, N, and Zn in the N-ZnO/FA/CQD with weight percent of 29%, 55%, 8%, and 1.5%, respectively. In the spectrum, the intense peak for Zn demonstrates the successful synthesis of ZnO nanorods. In addition, the presence of carbon and oxygen with high weight percent is due to the CQD and related functional groups in FA. Fig. 7a shows the fluorescence spectrum of the CQD. As shown, the spectrum has a characteristic absorption band at 450 nm. Visually, it can be seen that the CQDs are easily dispersed in water and the solution is transparent and yellow under normal light. However, it exhibits a blue-green fluorescence under UV light irradiation [39].

One of the most effective methods to define the light absorption capacity of a semiconductor at various wavelengths is UV–Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 7b shows the change in the absorbance of MB in the presence of different catalysts after the sonocatalytic degradation in the optimum conditions. The sharp adsorption peak at 668 nm is for MB. A comparison among the UV–Vis spectrum of MB in the presence of ZnO, CQD, ZnO/FA, and N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite shows a decrease in absorbance due to the degradation of dye. The efficiency of the degradation process was 94% in the presence of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite. The catalytic performance of N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was also extended for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) using 40 mL of 20 mg/L dye solution with the same experimental situation (Fig S2). The sharp adsorption peak at 554 nm is for RhB. The absorbance was reduced in the presence of catalyst. This confirms that N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite can be optimized for the efficient degradation of hazardous dyes other than MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study on MB degradation by different adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative investigation on the degradation efficiency of MB using ZnO, CQD, ZnO/FA and N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was performed in the presence of ultrasonic irradiation (US) for 5–50 min and the results are indicated in Fig. 7c. The investigation on the degradation efficiency of MB for all catalysts under similar conditions showed the following order: ZnO (20.1%)< CQD (40.6%)< ZnO/FA (77.6%)<N- ZnO/FA/CQD (94.5%). The presence of some functional groups on the surface of the ZnO/FA/CQDs catalyst improves its ability in the sonocatalytic degradation of MB compared to other synthesized nanoparticles. On the other hand, as can be seen from Fig. 7c, using ultrasonic alone has no significant effect on the degradation of methylene blue (MB) solution due to the inadequate amount of hydroxyl radicals and structurally complex and stable dye molecules. The sonication process can decompose water molecules to generate •OH and •H radicals. The degradation efficiency of MB with the addition of sonocatalyst was remarkably higher than that of sonication alone.

The improvement in degradation efficiency in the presence of catalysts may be related to the increasing number of cavitation bubbles formed on the surface of the sonocatalyst leading to more cleavage of water molecules and production of further •OH radicals. In addition, solid catalysts increase the mass transfer rate of MB molecules from liquid to the catalyst surface [40,41].

The pH of solution influences on the ionization of pollutants and the surface attributes of catalysts [23]. The effect of the solution pH on MB degradation efficiency is shown in Fig. 8a. The results showed that neutral and basic environments led to better efficacy than acidic environments. At acidic pH, H+ ions compete with cationic dye molecules for the adsorption onto the adsorbent surface. With increasing pH from 3 to 12, the interaction between cationic dye molecules and negatively charged sites (COO- in FA) formed in the basic environments increases which causes to more adsorption of the positive MB ions and probably increase in the degradation performance. The impact of pH on sonocatalytic degradation has been investigated in different studies. The changes in the chemical behavior of the dye molecules and catalyst surface charge in acidic and basic conditions are effective in degradation efficiency [36]. Since the increasing the pH from 8 to 12 causes to the slight changes in the MB degradation, the experiments were conducted in natural pH of the solution (pH 8).

The effect of MB initial concentration ([MB]0) on the efficacy of the sonocatalytic degradation process using N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 8b. The results show that the increase in the initial dye concentration from 10 to 40 mg/L causes to decrease in the degradation efficiency of the system; so it decreases from 92.2% to 52.7%. The increase in the initial MB concentration causes more active sites of the catalysts, which decreases the production of oxidizing radicals and the reduction in the rate of degradation process [39–42].

The effect of different concentrations of H2O2 (1–6 mM) on the degradation of the MB solution is illustrated in Fig. 8c. The results showed that only 47% of MB was removed in the absence of H2O2. By increasing the concentration of H2O2 from 1 to 5 mM, the MB degradation efficiency increases from 62% to 100%. The higher concentration of H2O2 produces more hydroxyl radicals (•OH) causing the increase in the degradation efficiency by N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite, but increase in H2O2 concentration higher than 5 mM has a detrimental effect. In literature, it has been reported that a high concentration of H2O2 causes a decrease in degradation efficiency through expenditure of formed hydroxyl radicals [43,44].

Fig. 8d shows the MB degradation efficiency as a function of contact time. The results indicate that with increasing the time from 5 to 50 min, the degradation efficiency increases with a linear trend and then become constant between 50 and 80 min. The maximum degradation efficiency of MB reaches equilibrium after 50 min. This is because of that the active sites of the catalyst are at first approximately free and available for dye molecules, and consequently, the degradation efficiency of MB increases. Finally, the active sites of the catalyst are gradually used, which reduces the degradation efficiency of MB [36].

The effect of different dosages of N-ZnO/FA/CQD (0.2–2 g/L) on the degradation of the MB solution is shown in Fig. 8e. Given these results, with increasing the catalyst dosage up to 1.5 g/L, the degradation efficiency increases, because the available active sites and generated hydroxyl radicals increase onto the catalyst surface [45]. The degradation efficiency reduces at concentrations higher than 1.5 g/L, due to compaction of the sonocatalyst nanoparticles leading to reducing the number of active sites as well as ultrasound scattering and blocking the transmission of ultrasound waves in the solution [46].

One of the important properties of adsorbents in the process of dye degradation is the stability and reusability of the adsorbent, especially for long-term usage. So, to evaluate the reusability of the catalyst, 1.5 g/L of N-ZnO/CQD/FA nanocomposite was used in four consecutive sonocatalytic cycles. Fig. 8f indicates that almost 15% loss of degradation efficiency was occurred after four runs.

Adsorption isotherms are used to explicit the surface properties and compare the adsorption capacities of the sorbents for pollutants in aqueous solutions. In this study, the Langmuir and Freundlich equations are used to describe the isotherms. The Langmuir model indicates the monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface without interaction between adsorbed molecules (Eq. (3)). The Freundlich isotherm model is used to explain the sorption on heterogeneous by interactions between adsorbed molecules surface (Eq. (4)) [47,48].
(3)
C
e
q
e
=
1
q
m
K
L
+
C
e
q
m
(4)
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
(
1 n
)
Where, Ce and qe are the equilibrium concentrations (mg/L) and MB adsorption capacity in the solution at equilibrium (mg/g), qm is the maximum amount of the adsorption capacity (mg/g), KL is the Langmuir isotherm constant (L/mg) and K F is a constant relating the sorption capacity and 1/n is an empirical parameter. The values of Langmuir constants qm and KL with the correlation coefficients for MB are presented in Table 2. From correlation coefficients obtained for both Freundlich and Langmuir models, it can be concluded that the adsorption equilibrium data can be fitted into the Langmuir model.

In this work, pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetic models were used to evaluate the kinetic rate constants of MB degradation onto the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite. The pseudo-first-order and the pseudo-second-order equations are as follows, respectively, [49,50]:
(5)
ln
(
q
e
−
q
t
)
=
lnq
e
–
K
1
t
(6)
t /
q
t
= 1 /
K
2
q
e
2
+ t /
q
e
Where, C0 is the initial concentration of dye, and C is the concentration at a certain time (min). K1 (min−1) and K2 (g/mg min) are the rates constant of pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetics, which can be calculated from the slope of the linear plot of log (qe-qt) versus t and the intercept of t/qe versus t, respectively [49]. Kinetic parameters for degradation of MB onto N-ZnO/FA/CQD are summarized in Table 3. According to the results, the correlation coefficient (R2) and qe values obtained for the pseudo-second-order kinetic model are better than that of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model.

In this study, the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite was successfully prepared by hydrothermal and green synthesis method for sonocatalytic degradation of MB in aqueous environments. The natural materials such as saffron and FA were used in the synthesis of eco-friendly catalyst. The results indicate that the N-ZnO/FA/CQD nanocomposite presents better sonocatalytic performance than pure ZnO nanorod, CQD, and ZnO/FA. The optimum conditions for sonocatalytic degradation of MB including ultrasonic irradiation time, solution pH, MB initial concentration, and catalyst dosage were 50 min, 8, 20 mg/L, and 1.5 g/L, respectively. At these conditions, the degradation of MB was obtained 94%. Moreover, the results indicated that the N-ZnO/FA/CQD has the highest degradation efficiency over the natural pH of the dye solution (pH 8).

Monire Moalem-Banhangi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Nayereh Ghaeni: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shahram Ghasemi: Supervision, Validation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - reviewing & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116626.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ fabrication of Mn3O4 decorated graphene oxide as a synergistic catalyst for degradation of methylene blue

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Y., Qu, J., Gao, F., Lv, S., Shi, L., He, C., & Sun, J. (2015). In situ fabrication of Mn3O4 decorated graphene oxide as a synergistic catalyst for degradation of methylene blue. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 162, 268‐274.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High performance visible-light responsive Chl-Cu/ZnO catalysts for photodegradation of rhodamine B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Worathitanon, C., Jangyubol, K., Ruengrung, P., Donphai, W., Klysubun, W., Chanlek, N., & Chareonpanich, M. (2019). High performance visible-light responsive Chl-Cu/ZnO catalysts for photodegradation of rhodamine B. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 241, 359‐366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sonophotocatalytic degradation mechanisms of Rhodamine B dye via radicals generation by micro-and nano-particles of ZnO

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lops, C., Ancona, A., Di Cesare, K., Dumontel, B., Garino, N., Canavese, G., & Cauda, V. (2019). Sonophotocatalytic degradation mechanisms of Rhodamine B dye via radicals generation by micro-and nano-particles of ZnO. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 243, 629‐640.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of anammox biocathode in microbial desalination and wastewater treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kokabian, B., Gude, V. G., Smith, R., & Brooks, J. P. (2018). Evaluation of anammox biocathode in microbial desalination and wastewater treatment. Chemical Engineering Journal, 342, 410‐419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microbial desalination cells for improved performance in wastewater treatment, electricity production, and desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Luo, H., Xu, P., Roane, T. M., Jenkins, P. E., & Ren, Z. (2012). Microbial desalination cells for improved performance in wastewater treatment, electricity production, and desalination. Bioresource Technology, 105, 60‐66.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of methylene blue adsorption onto activated carbon prepared from Date Press Cake by low frequency ultrasound

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Heidarinejad, Z., Rahmanian, O., Fazlzadeh, M., & Heidari, M. (2018). Enhancement of methylene blue adsorption onto activated carbon prepared from Date Press Cake by low frequency ultrasound. Journal of Molecular Liquids, 264, 591‐599.
